quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Confidence interval for μ"?

options.text: μ = np; the expected number of successes in n trials
x̄ ± t*·(s/√n); gives a range of plausible values for the population mean based on sample data
Variance addition rules require independence; for dependent variables, covariance terms must be included
A measure of the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two quantitative variables; ranges from -1 to +1

quiz_questions.explanation: "Confidence interval for μ" is defined as: x̄ ± t*·(s/√n); gives a range of plausible values for the population mean based on sample data. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Margin of error (for means)"?

options.text: A phenomenon where an association between two variables reverses direction when data are combined across a lurking variable
Tests H₀: μ₁ = μ₂ (or μ₁ - μ₂ = 0) using t = (x̄₁ - x̄₂)/√(s₁²/n₁ + s₂²/n₂)
t*·(s/√n); the maximum likely difference between x̄ and μ at the stated confidence level
Tests H₀ that a categorical variable follows a specified distribution by comparing observed and expected counts

quiz_questions.explanation: "Margin of error (for means)" is defined as: t*·(s/√n); the maximum likely difference between x̄ and μ at the stated confidence level. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Interpretation of confidence level"?

options.text: s = √(Σresidual²/(n-2)); estimates the standard deviation of the residuals (σ) with n-2 degrees of freedom
The line ŷ = a + bx that minimizes the sum of squared residuals; passes through the point (x̄, ȳ)
Occurs when individuals selected for a sample cannot be contacted or refuse to participate, and non-respondents differ systematically from respondents
If we repeated the sampling process many times, C% of the resulting intervals would capture the true population mean

quiz_questions.explanation: "Interpretation of confidence level" is defined as: If we repeated the sampling process many times, C% of the resulting intervals would capture the true population mean. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Effect of sample size"?

options.text: Increasing n decreases the margin of error proportionally to 1/√n, producing a narrower (more precise) interval
A study in which the researcher deliberately imposes one or more treatments on subjects to observe the effect on a response variable
μ = np; the expected number of successes in n trials
Tests H₀ that a categorical variable follows a specified distribution by comparing observed and expected counts

quiz_questions.explanation: "Effect of sample size" is defined as: Increasing n decreases the margin of error proportionally to 1/√n, producing a narrower (more precise) interval. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Required sample size"?

options.text: df = k - 1, where k is the number of categories; determines the shape of the chi-square distribution
Retrospective studies look backward at existing data; prospective studies follow subjects forward in time from the start
n = (t*·s/ME)²; the sample size needed to achieve a desired margin of error, often requiring an initial pilot study to estimate s
A data point falling more than 1.5 × IQR below Q1 or above Q3, potentially indicating unusual observations or data errors

quiz_questions.explanation: "Required sample size" is defined as: n = (t*·s/ME)²; the sample size needed to achieve a desired margin of error, often requiring an initial pilot study to estimate s. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "x̄ ± t*·(s/√n); gives a range of plausible values for the population mean based on sample data"?

options.text: Interpretation of slope CI
Standardized conditions
Confidence interval for μ
Type II error

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Confidence interval for μ".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "t*·(s/√n); the maximum likely difference between x̄ and μ at the stated confidence level"?

options.text: Confounding variable
Margin of error (for means)
Two-proportion z-test
Reading regression output

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Margin of error (for means)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "If we repeated the sampling process many times, C% of the resulting intervals would capture the true population mean"?

options.text: Independent events
Interpretation of confidence level
Variance (s²)
Logarithmic transformation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Interpretation of confidence level".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Increasing n decreases the margin of error proportionally to 1/√n, producing a narrower (more precise) interval"?

options.text: Response bias
Effect of sample size
Joint distribution
Percentile

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Effect of sample size".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "n = (t*·s/ME)²; the sample size needed to achieve a desired margin of error, often requiring an initial pilot study to estimate s"?

options.text: t-test for slope
Mean (x̄)
Percentile
Required sample size

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Required sample size".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Confidence Intervals for Means is accurate?

options.text: A well-designed experiment with random assignment allows researchers to attribute differences in response to the treatment rather than confounding variables.

The three major types—selection bias, response bias, and nonresponse bias—must be addressed through careful study design, while confounding variables create false associations that mask or mimic true causal relationships.
Confidence Intervals for Means estimate an unknown population mean μ using x̄ ± t*(s/√n), where t* comes from the t-distribution with df = n-1.

Correlation and Causation is a critical distinction in statistics: correlation measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship, but it does not imply that one variable causes changes in the other.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Confidence Intervals for Means as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Confidence Intervals for Means?

options.text: Linear Regression and Inference extends exploratory regression to formal inference about the population regression line β₀ + β₁x.
The width of the interval is determined by the confidence level (higher → wider), sample size (larger → narrower), and sample variability (larger s → wider).
Observational data can reveal associations but not causation, because confounding variables may drive the apparent relationship.

The binomial distribution counts the number of successes in a fixed number of independent trials with constant success probability: P(X=k) = C(n,k)p^k(1-p)^(n-k), with mean np and standard deviation √(np(1-p)).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Confidence Intervals for Means as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Confidence Intervals for Means?

options.text: Model comparison using r² and residual plots helps determine which model (linear, exponential, power) best fits the data.
Controlling Variables involves design strategies to isolate the effect of the treatment variable by holding other variables constant, matching subjects, or using statistical adjustments.
Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions describes the distribution of p̂ = X/n across all possible samples, where X is the count of successes in n independent trials.
Correct interpretation states: "We are C% confident that the true population mean lies within this interval," meaning C% of all such intervals capture μ.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Confidence Intervals for Means as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Confidence Intervals for Means?

options.text: Confidence Intervals for Means estimate an unknown population mean μ using x̄ ± t*(s/√n), where t* comes from the t-distribution with df = n-1.

Central Limit Theorem (CLT) states that regardless of the population's shape, the sampling distribution of x̄ approaches a normal distribution as sample size n increases (generally n ≥ 30 is sufficient).
Understanding this distinction is critical—many important questions (smoking's effect on health, educational interventions) rely on observational data when experiments are unethical or impractical.
This distribution forms the basis for inference about population proportions using z-procedures.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Confidence Intervals for Means as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Confidence Intervals for Means?

options.text: Chi-Square Test for Independence tests whether two categorical variables are associated in a single population.
Data collection methods—including census, survey, and experiment—each have distinct strengths and limitations that affect the validity and generalizability of conclusions.
One-sided tests (Hₐ: p > p₀ or p < p₀) and two-sided tests (Hₐ: p ≠ p₀) require different p-value calculations.
The width of the interval is determined by the confidence level (higher → wider), sample size (larger → narrower), and sample variability (larger s → wider).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Confidence Intervals for Means as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Means?

options.text: Manufacturing tolerance analysis using variance to ensure parts fall within acceptable dimensional limits
Classifying patient data in clinical trials as categorical (treatment group) versus quantitative (blood pressure reading) to select appropriate analysis methods
Hospital administrators constructing a 95% confidence interval for mean patient wait time to evaluate whether current staffing levels meet service standards
A/B testing at tech companies: testing whether a new feature increases click-through rate, with H₀: no difference and p-value determining rollout decisions

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Means.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Confidence Intervals for Means?

options.text: Public health research testing whether smoking status (smoker/non-smoker) is independent of lung disease status (yes/no) in a random sample of adults
Medical research blocking patients by disease severity before randomly assigning drug vs. placebo within each severity block
Consumer Reports testing batteries and reporting the mean lifetime as a confidence interval: '425 to 475 hours at 95% confidence'
Quality control using CLT to construct control charts for process means even when individual measurements follow a skewed distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Means.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Confidence Intervals for Means applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: FDA drug approval requiring p < 0.05 evidence that a new medication performs better than placebo before allowing it on the market
Agricultural researchers estimating average crop yield per acre using field trial data to determine whether a new variety outperforms the current standard
Medical screening: computing the probability of getting at least one false positive in multiple independent tests using the complement rule: P(at least one) = 1 - P(none)
Economists checking LINE conditions before publishing a regression model of how education level predicts income

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Means.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Confidence Intervals for Means?

options.text: Tobacco litigation using decades of observational evidence (cohort studies, dose-response relationships) to establish smoking as a cause of lung cancer when experiments were unethical
Hospital administrators constructing a 95% confidence interval for mean patient wait time to evaluate whether current staffing levels meet service standards
Polling organizations reporting margin of error, which depends on the standard error σ/√n, explaining why larger polls are more precise
A nutritionist testing whether the mean sodium content of a product exceeds 500 mg using a sample of 15 packages and a one-sample t-test

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Means.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Confidence Intervals for Means in practice?

options.text: Combining manufacturing tolerances: if a bolt and nut are produced independently, the play in the assembled joint has variance equal to the sum of their individual variances
Modeling population growth of bacteria over time using exponential transformation, then predicting colony size at future time points
Spam email filtering using conditional probability to classify messages based on the frequency of certain words in spam vs. legitimate emails (Bayesian classification)
Consumer Reports testing batteries and reporting the mean lifetime as a confidence interval: '425 to 475 hours at 95% confidence'

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Means.
